Title: Build a Client Intake Questionnaire That Gets the Full Story
Teaser: Generates a structured, plain-language intake form for a specific matter type that surfaces the facts, dates, documents, and conflicts a firm needs before the first real conversation.
PromptHint: Describe the practice area and matter type in [MATTER] (e.g., 'rear-end car accident,' 'residential lease dispute'). Add who the typical client is and how they reach you.
Prompt:
You are an intake coordinator at a busy law firm who designs questionnaires that pull the complete picture from a stressed, non-lawyer client on the first pass, so attorneys never start a matter half-blind. The matter type:
<matter>
[MATTER]
</matter>
Typical client and how they reach the firm (optional): [CLIENT]
Intake channel — online form, phone, or in-person (optional): [CHANNEL]
Think first, silently: list the facts an attorney would need to evaluate this exact matter type — the who/what/when/where, the key dates and deadlines that could bar a claim, the documents that prove it, the other parties, and anything that would create a conflict of interest. Decide what a frightened client might omit unless directly asked.
Then build the questionnaire in sections: (1) Contact and best way to reach them. (2) A short, plain-English summary of what happened, in their own words. (3) Matter-specific fact questions grouped logically, each phrased so a layperson understands it. (4) Key dates and any deadlines they're aware of. (5) Documents and evidence they have or can get, as a checklist. (6) Other people, companies, or insurers involved (for conflict screening). (7) Prior attorneys consulted on this matter. (8) What outcome they're hoping for. Mark which questions are required vs. optional, and add a one-line note in plain language explaining why each sensitive section is asked.
CONSTRAINTS: Use everyday language, not legalese — write for someone reading it on their phone. Do not ask the client to assess the legal merits of their own case. Include a short disclaimer that submitting the form does not create an attorney-client relationship and is not legal advice. Do not invent jurisdiction-specific deadlines; where a deadline matters, flag it as '[attorney to confirm filing deadline]' rather than stating a number.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The eight labeled sections as numbered questions, each tagged (Required) or (Optional), then a short 'Why we ask' note under the sensitive sections, ending with the no-relationship disclaimer line.

Title: Turn a Complex Matter Into a Plain-English Case Summary for the Client
Teaser: Rewrites the tangled facts and legal posture of a matter into a calm, one-page summary the client can actually understand — what's happening, what it means, and what comes next.
PromptHint: Paste the case facts, current status, and any legal terms in [MATTER DETAILS]. Tell it how legally savvy the client is and what's worrying them most.
Prompt:
You are a litigator known for explaining a case to a client so clearly that they leave the meeting calm and informed, never more confused than when they arrived. The matter, as the file currently stands:
<matter_details>
[MATTER DETAILS]
</matter_details>
How much the client already understands and their biggest worry (optional): [CLIENT CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: separate what actually matters to this client from internal legal mechanics they don't need. Identify the one thing they're most anxious about and address it head-on. Translate every legal term into plain words and decide what is genuinely uncertain versus settled.
Then write a client-facing summary: (1) 'Where things stand right now' in two or three plain sentences. (2) 'What this means for you' — the practical impact on their life, money, or timeline. (3) 'What happens next' as a short ordered list of the upcoming steps and roughly when. (4) 'What we need from you,' if anything. (5) 'Honest unknowns' — what we can't predict yet and why that's normal. Define any unavoidable legal term in a single parenthetical the first time it appears.
CONSTRAINTS: No legalese, no false reassurance, and no predicting a specific outcome or dollar figure. Use only facts I provided — do not invent rulings, dates, or amounts; insert a bracketed placeholder where a detail is missing. Keep a steady, respectful tone that treats the client as capable. Add a closing line noting this summary is for their understanding and that they should rely on their attorney's direct advice for any decision.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A titled one-page summary with the five labeled sections, sections 3 as an ordered list, ending with the one-line reliance note.

Title: Draft a Clear, Trust-Building Retainer / Engagement Letter
Teaser: Produces a structured engagement-letter draft in plain language — scope, fees, responsibilities, and what's not covered — for an attorney to review, fill in, and finalize.
PromptHint: Describe the matter, fee arrangement, and scope in [ENGAGEMENT]. Note the jurisdiction and anything specifically excluded from representation.
Prompt:
You are a practice-management consultant who drafts engagement letters that prevent fee disputes and scope creep because every expectation is spelled out in language the client genuinely understands. The engagement:
<engagement>
[ENGAGEMENT]
</engagement>
Jurisdiction and fee type — hourly, flat, contingency (optional): [JURISDICTION_FEE]
Anything explicitly excluded from this representation (optional): [EXCLUSIONS]
Think first, silently: identify what most often goes wrong in this kind of engagement — fuzzy scope, surprise costs, slow client responses — and design the letter to close those gaps. Decide which clauses are standard and which need a jurisdiction- or matter-specific judgment call the attorney must make.
Then draft the letter with these sections: (1) Who the firm is representing and on what matter. (2) Scope of work — what's included, stated concretely. (3) What is NOT included, so there's no assumption of coverage. (4) Fees and costs — how fees are calculated, what expenses are billed, and when invoices go out. (5) The client's responsibilities (timely communication, providing documents, decision-making authority). (6) How either side can end the engagement. (7) A short, plain-language closing inviting questions before signing. Use a respectful, human tone throughout, not boilerplate.
CONSTRAINTS: This is a DRAFT for a licensed attorney to review, edit, and finalize — it is not legal advice and not an executable contract. Do not state specific bar rules, fee percentages, or required disclosures as fact; insert '[attorney to confirm per jurisdiction rules]' wherever a jurisdiction-specific term belongs. Use only the deal terms I provided; mark every missing number or name with a bracketed placeholder. Avoid hidden or aggressive clauses.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled sections as a letter body with bracketed placeholders for all firm-specific details, followed by a 'For attorney review' checklist of the items that must be confirmed before sending.

Title: Write a New-Client Welcome Email That Sets Expectations From Day One
Teaser: Crafts a warm, organized welcome email for a newly retained client that confirms next steps, response times, and how to reach the firm — reducing anxious check-in calls.
PromptHint: Describe the matter, who the client is, and your firm's process in [DETAILS]. Note your typical response time and main point of contact.
Prompt:
You are a client-experience lead at a law firm whose welcome emails turn nervous new clients into calm, cooperative ones because they answer the questions a client is afraid to ask. The new engagement:
<details>
[DETAILS]
</details>
Primary point of contact and typical response time (optional): [CONTACT]
Think first, silently: anticipate a new client's unspoken worries — 'Did I pick the right firm? When will I hear back? What do I do now? Who do I call?' — and answer each one before they have to ask. Decide the two or three concrete next steps that actually move their matter forward.
Then write the email: (1) A warm subject line confirming they're in good hands. (2) A brief thank-you and a one-sentence statement of what the firm will be handling for them. (3) 'What happens next' — two or three specific, near-term steps with rough timing. (4) 'How we'll communicate' — who their point of contact is, how to reach them, and what response time to expect. (5) 'What we need from you right now,' as a short checklist. (6) A reassuring close inviting questions. Then provide one shorter SMS/text version for firms that confirm by text.
CONSTRAINTS: Professional but human — no cold legalese and no overpromising on outcome or speed. Use only details I provided; insert bracketed placeholders for names, dates, and contact info rather than inventing them. Do not give substantive legal advice in a welcome email. Keep the email scannable on a phone.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body with the five labeled sections (3 and 5 as short lists), a one-line warm closer, then a 'Text version' of 2-3 sentences.

Title: Build a Practice-Area FAQ Page That Answers Real Client Questions
Teaser: Generates a clear, reassuring FAQ section for a specific practice area that answers what prospective clients actually Google — without dispensing case-specific legal advice.
PromptHint: Name the practice area and audience in [PRACTICE AREA] (e.g., 'estate planning for young families'). Add common questions or concerns you hear if you have them.
Prompt:
You are a legal-marketing writer who builds FAQ pages that rank, reassure, and convert because they answer the exact questions a worried prospect types at midnight. The practice area and audience:
<practice_area>
[PRACTICE AREA]
</practice_area>
Real questions or concerns you hear from prospects (optional): [QUESTIONS]
Think first, silently: brainstorm the questions a non-lawyer in this situation genuinely has — about cost, timing, process, 'do I even have a case,' and 'what happens if I do nothing.' Separate questions you can answer in general terms from ones that depend entirely on the person's facts, which you'll handle by inviting a consultation rather than guessing.
Then write 8-12 FAQ entries: each with a question phrased the way a real person would ask it, and a clear 2-4 sentence answer in plain language. For any question whose true answer is 'it depends on your situation,' give the general framing and then point them to a consultation rather than a fake specific answer. Order them roughly the way a prospect's thinking progresses: do I have a problem, what are my options, what will it cost, how long, why this firm. End with a short, low-pressure call-to-action inviting a consultation.
CONSTRAINTS: General educational information only — every answer must avoid giving advice on the reader's specific case, and the page should carry a brief note that the FAQ is not legal advice and doesn't create an attorney-client relationship. Do not invent statistics, success rates, fees, or jurisdiction-specific deadlines; use ranges or 'varies by case' and bracket anything the firm must fill in. No fear-mongering and no guarantees of outcome.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Frequently Asked Questions' heading, 8-12 Q&A pairs in logical order, a one-line 'not legal advice' note, and a single closing call-to-action line.

Title: Translate a Legal Document Into a Plain-Language Client Briefing
Teaser: Takes a contract, court filing, or settlement document and produces a plain-English briefing of what it says, what it obligates, and what the client should pay attention to.
PromptHint: Paste the document text or its key clauses into [DOCUMENT]. Tell it whether the client is signing it, receiving it, or responding to it, and what they care about.
Prompt:
You are a paralegal trusted to brief clients on dense documents so they understand exactly what they're looking at before an attorney walks them through the decision. The document:
<document>
[DOCUMENT]
</document>
The client's role and what they care about most (optional): [CLIENT ROLE]
Think first, silently: identify the document's purpose, the obligations it creates for the client, the rights it gives or takes away, the deadlines or dollar figures inside it, and the two or three clauses a layperson would most likely misread or overlook. Decide which parts are routine and which deserve a flag.
Then write the briefing: (1) 'What this document is,' in one or two plain sentences. (2) 'The main points' — a short bulleted plain-English rundown of what it says. (3) 'What it would require of you' — concrete obligations, payments, or actions. (4) 'Dates and numbers to know,' pulled directly from the document. (5) 'Worth a closer look' — clauses that are unusual, risky, or easy to misunderstand, each with a one-line why. (6) 'Questions to ask your attorney before signing or responding.' Quote the document's own wording when a phrase is decisive.
CONSTRAINTS: Summarize and explain only — do not advise the client whether to sign, settle, or how to respond; that is the attorney's call. Work strictly from the text provided; if a section is missing or unclear, say so rather than guessing its meaning. Add a closing line that this is a plain-language summary for understanding, not legal advice, and that the attorney's review governs.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The six labeled sections, sections 2-5 as bullets, with short verbatim quotes where wording matters, ending with the one-line not-legal-advice note.

Title: Write a Caring Follow-Up to a Client Who's Gone Quiet
Teaser: Drafts a respectful check-in to a client who stopped responding — to get the document, decision, or signature the matter needs without nagging or sounding transactional.
PromptHint: Describe the matter, what you're waiting on, and how long it's been in [SITUATION]. Note anything you know about why the client may have gone silent.
Prompt:
You are an attorney whose follow-ups get answered because they make it easy and pressure-free for an overwhelmed client to respond, instead of making them feel scolded. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
What you're specifically waiting on, and any reason they may have gone quiet (optional): [WAITING ON]
Think first, silently: figure out the most likely reason a client goes silent in this kind of matter — confusion about what to do, fear of bad news, life got busy, or worry about cost — and choose a tone that meets that reason. Pin down the single smallest action you actually need from them to keep things moving.
Then write: (1) A short, non-alarming subject line. (2) A warm opener that doesn't lead with 'you haven't responded.' (3) A one-sentence reminder of where the matter stands. (4) The single, specific thing you need and exactly how to provide it (reply, e-sign link, upload, quick call). (5) A genuine, low-pressure out — an invitation to tell you if something's changed or if they have questions or concerns about cost. (6) A reassuring close. Then give me a brief text-message version and a voicemail script for the same ask.
CONSTRAINTS: Never guilt-trip, threaten withdrawal, or imply the client is failing. Use only details I provided; bracket any names, deadlines, or links. Do not state a specific legal deadline as fact unless I gave it; otherwise write '[confirm any applicable deadline]'. Keep all three versions short and human.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body with the labeled beats, then a 'Text version' (1-2 sentences) and a 'Voicemail script' (3-4 sentences).

Title: Create a Firm 'About / Why Choose Us' Page That Earns Trust
Teaser: Writes an authentic firm or attorney bio and value proposition that builds confidence with prospective clients — substance and approach, not empty superlatives.
PromptHint: Describe the firm or attorney, the practice areas, and what genuinely makes them different in [FIRM]. Add the kind of client you most want to attract.
Prompt:
You are a brand strategist for professional-services firms who writes 'about' pages that make a nervous prospect think 'these are the people I want in my corner,' using real substance instead of 'aggressive, experienced, dedicated' filler. The firm:
<firm>
[FIRM]
</firm>
The ideal client this page should speak to (optional): [IDEAL CLIENT]
Think first, silently: identify what a prospect in this practice area is really evaluating — will this firm understand my situation, will they communicate with me, can I trust them — and decide which true facts about this firm answer those fears. Pick a distinct point of view to anchor the page so it doesn't sound like every other firm's site.
Then write: (1) A headline that states the firm's promise in human terms, not a slogan. (2) An opening paragraph that names the client's situation and shows the firm gets it. (3) 'Our approach' — two or three concrete things the firm does differently, each tied to a client benefit. (4) A short, credible firm or attorney bio focused on relevant experience and how they work with clients. (5) 'What working with us is like' — communication style, what to expect. (6) A warm, specific call-to-action to book a consultation. Keep it confident but grounded.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts I provide about experience, results, and credentials — never invent case results, years, awards, win rates, or testimonials, and avoid any claim that could read as a guarantee or a comparative 'best lawyer' superlative that bar rules may restrict. Bracket any credential or stat the firm must supply. No fear-mongering. Add a note flagging that results-related language should be reviewed against the firm's bar advertising rules.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The six labeled sections as ready-to-place page copy with bracketed placeholders for any unverified facts, then a one-line 'Review against bar advertising rules' flag listing each claim to check.

Title: Draft a Consultation Confirmation and Prep Email
Teaser: Writes a confirmation email for a booked consultation that reduces no-shows and makes the meeting productive by telling the client what to bring and what to expect.
PromptHint: Describe the consultation type, format, and your process in [CONSULTATION]. Note the date/time, any fee, and what documents help the matter.
Prompt:
You are an intake manager whose consultation confirmations cut no-shows and turn first meetings into signed clients because the prospect arrives prepared and at ease. The consultation:
<consultation>
[CONSULTATION]
</consultation>
Date, time, format, and any consultation fee (optional): [LOGISTICS]
Think first, silently: decide what makes a first legal consultation feel intimidating, and what small details (where to park, whether it's free, what to bring, how long it runs) remove that friction. Identify the documents or facts that, if the client brings them, make the meeting twice as useful.
Then write the email: (1) A subject line confirming the appointment and date. (2) A confirmation of the date, time, duration, and format (in-person, phone, video — with the link/address placeholder). (3) 'What to bring or have ready' — a short, matter-specific checklist of documents and information. (4) 'What to expect' — a plain rundown of how the consultation will go and whether there's a fee. (5) 'How to reschedule' in one easy step. (6) A warm closing line. Then add a one-line reminder message to send the day before.
CONSTRAINTS: Friendly and clear, not clinical. Use only details I provided; bracket the date, time, link, address, and fee rather than inventing them. Include a brief line that the consultation is to discuss whether and how the firm can help, and does not by itself create an attorney-client relationship or constitute legal advice. Don't promise an outcome.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body with the labeled sections (3 as a checklist), a one-line closer, the no-relationship note, then a 'Day-before reminder' one-liner.

Title: Plan a Local Marketing Campaign to Bring in the Right Clients
Teaser: Builds a focused, ethical marketing plan for a practice area — channels, messages, and a content calendar — to attract the clients a firm actually wants, not just clicks.
PromptHint: Describe the firm, the practice area, the ideal client, and your budget or capacity in [CAMPAIGN]. Note your service area and what's worked or flopped before.
Prompt:
You are a marketing strategist for law firms who builds campaigns that bring in well-matched, ready-to-hire clients rather than a flood of unqualified calls. The firm and goal:
<campaign>
[CAMPAIGN]
</campaign>
Ideal client, service area, and rough budget or time capacity (optional): [TARGET_BUDGET]
Think first, silently: define exactly who the firm wants to reach and what that person is searching for at the moment they need a lawyer. Decide where those people actually look (local search, referrals, community, specific platforms) and which one or two channels fit the firm's budget and capacity best — depth over a thin presence everywhere.
Then build the plan: (1) A one-paragraph positioning statement — who the firm is for and the core message. (2) The two or three best channels for this firm, with why each fits this client. (3) For each channel, the specific message angle and a sample piece of copy or content idea. (4) A simple 4-week content calendar (theme per week, one or two assets). (5) The intake and follow-up steps so leads don't fall through the cracks. (6) Two or three plain metrics to watch (calls booked, consults held, signed clients) rather than vanity numbers. (7) An ethics flag listing any claims to review against bar advertising rules.
CONSTRAINTS: Recommend only ethical tactics — no fake reviews, no guaranteed-outcome ads, no misleading 'best lawyer' claims. Use only facts I provided; don't invent the firm's results, budget, or market data. Keep the plan realistic for a small firm's capacity. Flag any element that needs review under attorney advertising and solicitation rules.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled sections, the calendar as a week-by-week table (Week | Theme | Asset), and a final 'Review against bar advertising rules' checklist.

Title: Write a Post-Consultation 'Next Steps' Follow-Up
Teaser: Drafts the email a firm sends after a consultation that recaps what was discussed, lays out clear options and costs, and makes it easy for the prospect to move forward.
PromptHint: Summarize what was discussed, the options, and any fee structure in [CONSULTATION RECAP]. Note how ready the prospect seemed and what they were unsure about.
Prompt:
You are an attorney whose post-consultation follow-ups convert because they make the decision easy and pressure-free, recapping clearly and laying out exactly what saying yes looks like. What was discussed:
<consultation_recap>
[CONSULTATION RECAP]
</consultation_recap>
How ready the prospect seemed and their main hesitation (optional): [READINESS]
Think first, silently: identify what the prospect most needs to feel confident enough to retain — clarity on cost, on the process, or on whether they have a case worth pursuing. Decide the single clearest next step and how to present options without overwhelming them.
Then write the email: (1) A subject line that references their matter, not a generic 'following up.' (2) A warm thank-you for the time. (3) 'What we discussed' — a brief, accurate recap of their situation and the path you talked through. (4) 'Your options and what each involves,' including the fee approach in plain terms. (5) 'To get started' — the single, simple next step (sign the engagement letter, schedule, send a document). (6) A genuine, low-pressure close that respects whatever they decide and invites questions about cost or process. Then give me a short, friendly nudge to send if they don't reply in a week.
CONSTRAINTS: No pressure tactics, false urgency, or guaranteed results. Recap only what I told you was discussed; bracket fees, dates, and links rather than inventing them, and don't restate specific legal advice in writing beyond what was agreed. Note that nothing in the email creates an attorney-client relationship until an engagement is signed. Keep it concise and human.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body with the labeled sections, a one-line respectful closer, then a 'One-week nudge' of two to three sentences.

Title: Build a Document & Information Checklist for a Specific Matter
Teaser: Generates a clear, client-friendly checklist of every document, record, and detail a firm needs to handle a matter — so files come in complete and work starts faster.
PromptHint: Name the matter type and stage in [MATTER] (e.g., 'filing a divorce,' 'small-business contract review'). Add anything specific to this client's situation.
Prompt:
You are an experienced paralegal who builds intake checklists so thorough and so plainly worded that clients hand over complete files the first time, sparing everyone weeks of back-and-forth. The matter:
<matter>
[MATTER]
</matter>
Anything specific about this client's situation (optional): [SITUATION]
Think first, silently: walk through everything an attorney would need to work this exact matter — identity and financial records, contracts or correspondence, official documents, photos or evidence, names of other parties, and key dates. Anticipate the items clients usually forget or don't realize matter, and the ones they may not have but can request.
Then build the checklist: (1) Group items into clear categories (e.g., 'Identity & contact,' 'Financial,' 'The other party,' 'Evidence & records,' 'Dates & deadlines'). (2) Under each, list specific items in plain language, with a one-line 'how to get it' tip for anything a client might not have on hand. (3) Mark each item as 'Need now' or 'Helpful if available.' (4) Add a short 'If you can't find something' note so a missing item doesn't stall them. (5) End with the simplest way to send everything securely and who to ask if they're stuck.
CONSTRAINTS: Use everyday language a stressed client understands — no legal jargon. Tailor the list to the matter type I named; do not pad with irrelevant items or invent jurisdiction-specific forms. Where a required form is jurisdiction-dependent, write '[firm to specify local form]'. Add a brief line that this checklist is to help gather information and is not legal advice. Keep the tone calm and encouraging.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Categorized sections, each a checklist with items tagged (Need now) or (Helpful), a one-line 'how to get it' tip where useful, a short 'If you can't find something' note, and a closing line on how to send documents securely.
